children.subject: Re:
children.body:
Shrek has been a foundation of America for many years. I feel as if Shrek
represents our great nation more than a mere eagle. It would set an example
for the rest of the international community to choose something that
represents their countries as a whole, a change for the better. The people
of America deserve more then a bird, they deserve the symbol of
free-thinking prosperity. Shrek is the true epitome of the American dream.
Shrek is not white, not asian, not black, Shrek represents true diversity
by being green. For America to take up the mantle as a bastion of diversity
and free thinking, Shrek MUST become the national bird. Some may say "But
Shrek is not a bird" BUT, that represents the true meaning of progressive
acceptance. Shrek may identify as a bird, and we must honour that. I plead
to you, fellow Americans, make this country as great as the values it
stands for.

>
> Shrek has been a foundation of America for many years. I feel as if Shrek
> represents our great nation more than a mere eagle. It would set an example
> for the rest of the international community to choose something that
> represents their countries as a whole, a change for the better. The people
> of America deserve more then a bird, they deserve the symbol of
> free-thinking prosperity. Shrek is the true epitome of the American dream.
> Shrek is not white, not asian, not black, Shrek represents true diversity
> by being green. For America to take up the mantle as a bastion of diversity
> and free thinking, Shrek MUST become the national bird. Some may say "But
> Shrek is not a bird" BUT, that represents the true meaning of progressive
> acceptance. Shrek may identify as a bird, and we must honour that. I plead
> to you, fellow Americans, make this country as great as the values it
> stands for.
>
>

children.subject: Re:
children.body:
what the lsam



Shrek has been a foundation of America for many years. I feel as if Shrek
represents our great nation more than a mere eagle. It would set an example
for the rest of the international community to choose something that
represents their countries as a whole, a change for the better. The people
of America deserve more then a bird, they deserve the symbol of
free-thinking prosperity. Shrek is the true epitome of the American dream.
Shrek is not white, not asian, not black, Shrek represents true diversity
by being green. For America to take up the mantle as a bastion of diversity
and free thinking, Shrek MUST become the national bird. Some may say "But
Shrek is not a bird" BUT, that represents the true meaning of progressive
acceptance. Shrek may identify as a bird, and we must honour that. I plead
to you, fellow Americans, make this country as great as the values it
stands for.

subject: Ap test
body:
Dear Dr.Nair,

This is Sreekar Charan.
Hope you are doing well.
I just woke up and realized my school issued computer is missing.
Am I allowed to use someone else’s computer for the AP test provided I sign
in to my user?
If not, is there anyway I could get a loaner-computer?
Thank you.
Best regards, Sreekar Charan

children.subject: Re: Ap test
children.body:
What do you mean, your school issued laptop is missing? You have been taking mock exams in my class the past few weeks. We did Station Rotations to review AP biology on Friday... did you use your laptop in any other class? Go and check in that classroom as soon as you get to school tomorrow... Bring your charger with you.




Jyoti Nair, Ph.D.

AP Biology Teacher

Chess Club & GIDAS Club Sponsor

480-883-5146

Hamilton High School

Home of the Huskies



________________________________

children.subject: Re: Ap test
children.body:
Don't forget to charge it, and bring it tomorrow with a charger... Go and look for your laptop first thing in the morning.




Jyoti Nair, Ph.D.

AP Biology Teacher

Chess Club & GIDAS Club Sponsor

480-883-5146

Hamilton High School

Home of the Huskies



________________________________
From: Sreekar Charan <s353566@gse.cusd80.com>
Sent: Sunday, May 4, 2025 11:10 AM
To: Nair, Jyoti <nair.jyoti@cusd80.com>
Subject: Re: Ap test

I signed in and it works.
Best regards,
Sreekar Charan

On Sun, 4 May 2025 at 11:05 AM, Nair, Jyoti <nair.jyoti@cusd80.com<mailto:nair.jyoti@cusd80.com>> wrote:
Is your friend's laptop issued from school? Does he have Bluebook App downloaded and updated? You may want to sign in and check to see if it works.

Bring it in any case.




Jyoti Nair, Ph.D.

AP Biology Teacher

Chess Club & GIDAS Club Sponsor

480-883-5146

Hamilton High School

Home of the Huskies



________________________________
From: Sreekar Charan <s353566@gse.cusd80.com<mailto:s353566@gse.cusd80.com>>
Sent: Sunday, May 4, 2025 11:02 AM
To: Nair, Jyoti <nair.jyoti@cusd80.com<mailto:nair.jyoti@cusd80.com>>
Subject: Re: Ap test

I know I had it in my last period on Friday. I will check there before school. Worst case scenario though I have a friends computer I can use—- is that allowed as long as I sign in to my user?.
Best regards,
Sreekar Charan
On Sun, 4 May 2025 at 10:59 AM, Nair, Jyoti <nair.jyoti@cusd80.com<mailto:nair.jyoti@cusd80.com>> wrote:
What do you mean, your school issued laptop is missing? You have been taking mock exams in my class the past few weeks. We did Station Rotations to review AP biology on Friday... did you use your laptop in any other class? Go and check in that classroom as soon as you get to school tomorrow... Bring your charger with you.




Jyoti Nair, Ph.D.

AP Biology Teacher

Chess Club & GIDAS Club Sponsor

480-883-5146

Hamilton High School

Home of the Huskies



________________________________
From: Sreekar Charan <s353566@gse.cusd80.com<mailto:s353566@gse.cusd80.com>>
Sent: Sunday, May 4, 2025 10:30 AM
To: Jyoti Nair <nair.jyoti@gse.cusd80.com<mailto:nair.jyoti@gse.cusd80.com>>
Subject: Ap test


[CAUTION]: This email originated from outside our domain CUSD80.COM<http://CUSD80.COM>


Dear Dr.Nair,

This is Sreekar Charan.
Hope you are doing well.
I just woke up and realized my school issued computer is missing.
Am I allowed to use someone else’s computer for the AP test provided I sign in to my user?
If not, is there anyway I could get a loaner-computer?
Thank you.
Best regards, Sreekar Charan

________________________________

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_Everyone_stop_dragging_this_email_meme_it=E2=80=99s_no?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?t_funny_anymore?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

	=?UTF-8?Q?t_funny_anymore?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

children.body:
be quiet raymog




*Men are born for games. Nothing else. Every child knows that play is
nobler than work. He knows too that the worth or merit of a game is not
inherent in the game itself but rather in the value of that which is put at
hazard. Games of chance require a wager to have meaning at all. Games of
sport involve the skill and strength of the opponents and the humiliation
of defeat and the pride of victory are in themselves sufficient stake
because they inhere in the worth of the principals and define them. But the
trial of chance or trial of worth all games aspire to the condition of war
for here that which is wagered swallows up game, player, all. **Suppose two
men at cards with nothing to wager save their lives. Who has not heard such
a tale? A turn of the card. The whole universe for such a player has
labored clanking to this moment which will tell if he is to die at that
man’s hand or that man at his. What more certain validation of a man’s
worth could there be? This enhancement of the game to its ultimate state
admits no argument concerning the notion of fate. The selection of one man
over another is a preference absolute and irrevocable and it is a dull man
indeed who could reckon so profound a decision without agency or
significance either one. In such games as have for their stake the
annihilation of the defeated the decisions are quite clear. This man
holding this particular arrangement of cards in his hand is thereby removed
from existence.*

bro this is the email villain arc, now i know how it feels to be bombarded
with emails





It is far better to live like a lion for a day than to live like a jackal
for a hundred years.

> nobler than work. He knows too that the worth or merit of a game is not
> inherent in the game itself but rather in the value of that which is put at
> hazard. Games of chance require a wager to have meaning at all. Games of
> sport involve the skill and strength of the opponents and the humiliation
> of defeat and the pride of victory are in themselves sufficient stake
> because they inhere in the worth of the principals and define them. But the
> trial of chance or trial of worth all games aspire to the condition of war
> for here that which is wagered swallows up game, player, all. **Suppose
> two men at cards with nothing to wager save their lives. Who has not heard
> such a tale? A turn of the card. The whole universe for such a player has
> labored clanking to this moment which will tell if he is to die at that
> man’s hand or that man at his. What more certain validation of a man’s
> worth could there be? This enhancement of the game to its ultimate state
> admits no argument concerning the notion of fate. The selection of one man
> over another is a preference absolute and irrevocable and it is a dull man
> indeed who could reckon so profound a decision without agency or
> significance either one. In such games as have for their stake the
> annihilation of the defeated the decisions are quite clear. This man
> holding this particular arrangement of cards in his hand is thereby removed
> from existence.*

	=?UTF-8?Q?t_funny_anymore?=
children.body:
LSam email




*Men are born for games. Nothing else. Every child knows that play is
nobler than work. He knows too that the worth or merit of a game is not
inherent in the game itself but rather in the value of that which is put at
hazard. Games of chance require a wager to have meaning at all. Games of
sport involve the skill and strength of the opponents and the humiliation
of defeat and the pride of victory are in themselves sufficient stake
because they inhere in the worth of the principals and define them. But the
trial of chance or trial of worth all games aspire to the condition of war
for here that which is wagered swallows up game, player, all. **Suppose two
men at cards with nothing to wager save their lives. Who has not heard such
a tale? A turn of the card. The whole universe for such a player has
labored clanking to this moment which will tell if he is to die at that
man’s hand or that man at his. What more certain validation of a man’s
worth could there be? This enhancement of the game to its ultimate state
admits no argument concerning the notion of fate. The selection of one man
over another is a preference absolute and irrevocable and it is a dull man
indeed who could reckon so profound a decision without agency or
significance either one. In such games as have for their stake the
annihilation of the defeated the decisions are quite clear. This man
holding this particular arrangement of cards in his hand is thereby removed
from existence.*

>>>> inherent in the game itself but rather in the value of that which is put at

>>>> of defeat and the pride of victory are in themselves sufficient stake
>>>> because they inhere in the worth of the principals and define them. But the
>>>> trial of chance or trial of worth all games aspire to the condition of war

>>>> admits no argument concerning the notion of fate. The selection of one man
>>>> over another is a preference absolute and irrevocable and it is a dull man
>>>> indeed who could reckon so profound a decision without agency or
>>>> significance either one. In such games as have for their stake the
>>>> annihilation of the defeated the decisions are quite clear. This man

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_Everyone_stop_dragging_this_email_meme_it=E2=80=99s_no?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?t_funny_anymore?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_Everyone_stop_dragging_this_email_meme_it=E2=80=99s_no?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?t_funny_anymore?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

children.subject: Re: PERRY HS GUITAR CLUB PASSES
children.body:
Shrek has been a foundation of America for many years. I feel as if Shrek
represents our great nation more than a mere eagle. It would set an example
for the rest of the international community to choose something that
represents their countries as a whole, a change for the better. The people
of America deserve more then a bird, they deserve the symbol of
free-thinking prosperity. Shrek is the true epitome of the American dream.
Shrek is not white, not asian, not black, Shrek represents true diversity
by being green. For America to take up the mantle as a bastion of diversity
and free thinking, Shrek MUST become the national bird. Some may say "But
Shrek is not a bird" BUT, that represents the true meaning of progressive
acceptance. Shrek may identify as a bird, and we must honour that. I plead
to you, fellow Americans, make this country as great as the values it
stands for.

children.subject: Re: PERRY HS GUITAR CLUB PASSES
children.body:
I've made a severe and continuous lapse in my judgement, and I don’t expect
to be forgiven, I'm simply here to apologize
<https://genius.com/28732338/Logan-paul-so-sorry/Ive-made-a-severe-and-continuous-lapse-in-my-judgement-and-i-dont-expect-to-be-forgiven-im-simply-here-to-apologize>
. So <https://genius.com/28732347/Logan-paul-so-sorry/So> what we came
across that day in the woods was obviously unplanned, and the reactions you
saw on tape were raw, they were unfiltered. None of us knew how to react,
or how to feel
<https://genius.com/28732341/Logan-paul-so-sorry/None-of-us-knew-how-to-react-or-how-to-feel>
. I should have never posted the video
<https://genius.com/28732312/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-should-have-never-posted-the-video>
. I should have put the cameras down and stopped recording what we were
going through
<https://genius.com/28732322/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-should-have-put-the-cameras-down-and-stopped-recording-what-we-were-going-through>
. <https://genius.com/28799536/Logan-paul-so-sorry/> There's a lot of
things I should have done differently but I didn’t, and for that, from the
bottom of my heart, I am sorry
<https://genius.com/28732325/Logan-paul-so-sorry/Theres-a-lot-of-things-i-should-have-done-differently-but-i-didnt-and-for-that-from-the-bottom-of-my-heart-i-am-sorry>
. I want to apologize to the Internet
<https://genius.com/28461979/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-want-to-apologize-to-the-internet>
. <https://genius.com/29324852/Logan-paul-so-sorry/> I want to apologize to
anyone who's seen the video
<https://genius.com/28732337/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-want-to-apologize-to-anyone-whos-seen-the-video>
. I want to apologize to anyone who has been affected or touched by mental
illness, or depression, or suicide, but most importantly, I want to
apologize to the victim and his family
<https://genius.com/28732331/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-want-to-apologize-to-anyone-who-has-been-affected-or-touched-by-mental-illness-or-depression-or-suicide-but-most-importantly-i-want-to-apologize-to-the-victim-and-his-family>
. <https://genius.com/28734374/Logan-paul-so-sorry/> For my fans who are
defending my actions, please don't
<https://genius.com/28732327/Logan-paul-so-sorry/For-my-fans-who-are-defending-my-actions-please-dont>
. They do not deserve to be defended
<https://genius.com/28732350/Logan-paul-so-sorry/They-do-not-deserve-to-be-defended>
. The goal with my content is always to entertain, to push the boundaries,
to be all inclusive in the world I live in
<https://genius.com/28732353/Logan-paul-so-sorry/The-goal-with-my-content-is-always-to-entertain-to-push-the-boundaries-to-be-all-inclusive-in-the-world-i-live-in>
. I share almost everything I do
<https://genius.com/28732355/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-share-almost-everything-i-do>
. The intent is never to be heartless, cruel, or malicious
<https://genius.com/28732368/Logan-paul-so-sorry/The-intent-is-never-to-be-heartless-cruel-or-malicious>
. Like I said, I've made a huge mistake
<https://genius.com/28732369/Logan-paul-so-sorry/Like-i-said-ive-made-a-huge-mistake>
. <https://genius.com/32619389/Logan-paul-so-sorry/> I don't expect to be
forgiven, I'm just here to apologize
<https://genius.com/28732376/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I-dont-expect-to-be-forgiven-im-just-here-to-apologize>
. I <https://genius.com/28732381/Logan-paul-so-sorry/I>'m ashamed of
myself. I'm disappointed in myself and I promise to be better. I will be
better. Thank you.
<https://genius.com/28461977/Logan-paul-so-sorry/M-ashamed-of-myself-im-disappointed-in-myself-and-i-promise-to-be-better-i-will-be-better-thank-you>

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_WHAT_UP_GANG=F0=9F=98=9B=F0=9F=98=9B_I_GO_TO_HAMILTON_BUT_ILL_JO?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?IN_YO_CLUB_TWIN?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_WHAT_UP_GANG=F0=9F=98=9B=F0=9F=98=9B_I_GO_TO_HAMILTON_BUT_ILL_JO?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?IN_YO_CLUB_TWIN?=
children.body:
On Tue, Aug 26, 2025 at 12:15 PM Raiden Fernandez <s432121@gse.cusd80.com>
wrote:

> Guitar strings and heartbreak
>
> The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
> Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
> drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
> casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.
>
>
> Amelia Torres.
>
> Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
> her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
> more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
> stomach did a backflip.
>
>
> “Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
> to start writing for the spring showcase.”
>
>
> Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
> through the air.
>
>
> “Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”
>
>
> Jaxon Reed.
>
> Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les
> Paul looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and
> the way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.
>
>
> “You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the
> back like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.
>
>
> The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
> Jaxon.”
>
>
> Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp
> chord that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You
> and me. Head-to-head.”
>
>
> Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.
>
>
> “A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
> showcase opener.”
>
>
> But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
> across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
> what he was really capable of.
>
>
> And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.
>
> It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.
>
> Part 2
>
> The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
> strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
> bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
> Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.
>
>
> It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.
>
> And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.
>
>
> She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
> Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
> he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
> confidence, every solo demanding attention.
>
>
> When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
> Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.
>
>
> “Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
> practicing instead of hiding.”
>
>
> Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.
>
> “That was… amazing. Both of you.”
>
>
> Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
> looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
> fade.
>
>
> “You’ve both got something special.”
>
>
> Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
> really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.
>
>
> “Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
> opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”
>
>
> Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
> but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”
>
>
> For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
> about music anymore—it was about her.
>
>
> As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
> walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
> in first.
>
>
> “Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
> Work on some songs.”
>
>
> Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.
>
>
> And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.
>
>
> A triangle.
>
> Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
> Part 3
>
> Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
> of Guitar Club.
>
> Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
> attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
> a clever chord or lyric.
>
>
> But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
> played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.
>
>
> And Amelia noticed.
>
>
> Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
> eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
> thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
> like the song was just for her.
>
>
> Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He
> started cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they
> could “own the showcase” if she stuck with him.
>
>
> But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
> blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
> the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
> flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.
>
>
> And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.
>
> ------------------------------
>
> The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
> Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
> crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.
>
>
> When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
> intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.
>
>
> He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
> This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
> always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
> them could’ve made alone.
>
>
> By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted
> in applause.
>
>
> But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.
>
> He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:
>
>
> “I knew you had this in you.”
>
>
> His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
> her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.
>
>
> It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
> songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.
>
>
> And for now, that was enough.
>
>
>
> On Tue, Aug 26, 2025 at 12:13 PM Aariz Khan <s360835@gse.cusd80.com>
> wrote:
>
>> i wont
>>
>>
>>
>>
>>
>> It is far better to live like a lion for a day than to live like a jackal
>> for a hundred years.
>>
>>

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_WHAT_UP_GANG=F0=9F=98=9B=F0=9F=98=9B_I_GO_TO_HAMILTON_BUT_ILL_JO?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?IN_YO_CLUB_TWIN?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.

>
> The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
> Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
> crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.
>
>
> When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
> intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.
>
>
> He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
> This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
> always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
> them could’ve made alone.
>
>
> By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted
> in applause.
>
>
> But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.
>
> He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:
>
>
> “I knew you had this in you.”
>
>
> His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
> her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.
>
>
> It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
> songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.
>
>
> And for now, that was enough.
>
>
>

>>> Guitar strings and heartbreak
>>>
>>> The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
>>> Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
>>> drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
>>> casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.
>>>
>>>
>>> Amelia Torres.
>>>
>>> Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic
>>> in her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to
>>> say more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down.
>>> His stomach did a backflip.
>>>
>>>
>>> “Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into
>>> groups to start writing for the spring showcase.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
>>> through the air.
>>>
>>>
>>> “Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Jaxon Reed.
>>>
>>> Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les
>>> Paul looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and
>>> the way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.
>>>
>>>
>>> “You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the
>>> back like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.
>>>
>>>
>>> The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
>>> Jaxon.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp
>>> chord that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You
>>> and me. Head-to-head.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.
>>>
>>>
>>> “A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
>>> showcase opener.”
>>>
>>>
>>> But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s
>>> gaze across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted
>>> to see what he was really capable of.
>>>
>>>
>>> And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.
>>>
>>> It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to
>>> himself.
>>>
>>> Part 2
>>>
>>> The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
>>> strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
>>> bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
>>> Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.
>>>
>>>
>>> It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.
>>>
>>> And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.
>>>
>>>
>>> She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
>>> Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
>>> he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
>>> confidence, every solo demanding attention.
>>>
>>>
>>> When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
>>> Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.
>>>
>>>
>>> “Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
>>> practicing instead of hiding.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.
>>>
>>> “That was… amazing. Both of you.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when
>>> she looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile
>>> didn’t fade.
>>>
>>>
>>> “You’ve both got something special.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
>>> really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.
>>>
>>>
>>> “Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write
>>> the opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear.
>>> “Maybe… but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”
>>>
>>>
>>> For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t
>>> just about music anymore—it was about her.
>>>
>>>
>>> As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
>>> walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
>>> in first.
>>>
>>>
>>> “Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
>>> Work on some songs.”
>>>
>>>
>>> Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.
>>>
>>>
>>> And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.
>>>
>>>
>>> A triangle.
>>>
>>> Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
>>> Part 3
>>>
>>> Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the
>>> heartbeat of Guitar Club.
>>>
>>> Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold
>>> Amelia’s attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress
>>> her with a clever chord or lyric.
>>>
>>>
>>> But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He
>>> just… played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.
>>>
>>>
>>> And Amelia noticed.
>>>
>>>
>>> Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch
>>> her eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out
>>> of thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice
>>> soft, like the song was just for her.
>>>
>>>
>>> Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He
>>> started cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they
>>> could “own the showcase” if she stuck with him.
>>>
>>>
>>> But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her
>>> voice blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone
>>> in the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
>>> flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.
>>>
>>>
>>> And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.
>>>
>>> ------------------------------
>>>
>>> The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with
>>> anticipation. Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud,
>>> unforgettable. The crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.
>>>
>>>
>>> When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big

>>> This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
>>> always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
>>> them could’ve made alone.
>>>
>>>
>>> By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted
>>> in applause.
>>>
>>>
>>> But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.
>>>
>>> He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing
>>> his:
>>>
>>>
>>> “I knew you had this in you.”
>>>
>>>
>>> His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause.
>>> Just her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.
>>>
>>>
>>> It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
>>> songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.
>>>
>>>
>>> And for now, that was enough.

children.body:
be quiet




*Men are born for games. Nothing else. Every child knows that play is
nobler than work. He knows too that the worth or merit of a game is not
inherent in the game itself but rather in the value of that which is put at
hazard. Games of chance require a wager to have meaning at all. Games of
sport involve the skill and strength of the opponents and the humiliation
of defeat and the pride of victory are in themselves sufficient stake
because they inhere in the worth of the principals and define them. But the
trial of chance or trial of worth all games aspire to the condition of war
for here that which is wagered swallows up game, player, all. **Suppose two
men at cards with nothing to wager save their lives. Who has not heard such
a tale? A turn of the card. The whole universe for such a player has
labored clanking to this moment which will tell if he is to die at that
man’s hand or that man at his. What more certain validation of a man’s
worth could there be? This enhancement of the game to its ultimate state
admits no argument concerning the notion of fate. The selection of one man
over another is a preference absolute and irrevocable and it is a dull man
indeed who could reckon so profound a decision without agency or
significance either one. In such games as have for their stake the
annihilation of the defeated the decisions are quite clear. This man
holding this particular arrangement of cards in his hand is thereby removed
from existence.*

children.subject:
=?UTF-8?Q?Re=3A_WHAT_UP_GANG=F0=9F=98=9B=F0=9F=98=9B_I_GO_TO_HAMILTON_BUT_ILL_JO?=
	=?UTF-8?Q?IN_YO_CLUB_TWIN?=
children.body:
Guitar strings and heartbreak

The room hummed with tension the moment Dylan Smith walked into Guitar
Club. His red Stratocaster gleamed under the flickering fluorescent lights,
drawing eyes whether he wanted them or not. He slung it over his shoulder
casually, but his heart was already racing—because she was there.


Amelia Torres.

Her voice was legendary around school, and even with just her acoustic in
her lap, she owned the room. Dylan had never worked up the courage to say
more than “hey” to her, but today she smiled at him when he sat down. His
stomach did a backflip.


“Alright,” Ms. Reyes clapped her hands. “We’re going to split into groups
to start writing for the spring showcase.”


Before Dylan could even move, a voice he really didn’t want to hear cut
through the air.


“Well, well. If it isn’t Smith.”


Jaxon Reed.

Tall, smug, and the self-proclaimed king of Guitar Club. His black Les Paul
looked like it had been ripped straight from a rock god’s hands, and the
way he smirked made Dylan’s skin crawl.


“You actually planning on playing this semester, or just hiding in the back
like last year?” Jaxon sneered, loud enough for everyone to hear.


The group chuckled nervously, but Amelia frowned at him. “Cut it out,
Jaxon.”


Dylan’s ears burned, but instead of backing down, he strummed a sharp chord
that echoed like a challenge. “Why don’t we settle it with music? You and
me. Head-to-head.”


Gasps rippled through the room. Even Ms. Reyes raised an eyebrow.


“A duel?” Jaxon grinned. “Fine. Winner gets bragging rights for the
showcase opener.”


But Dylan wasn’t just thinking about the showcase. He caught Amelia’s gaze
across the circle. Her eyes lit up—excited, curious, like she wanted to see
what he was really capable of.


And in that moment, Dylan knew this wasn’t just about beating Jaxon.

It was about proving himself—to the club, to Amelia, maybe even to himself.

Part 2

The music room was alive with sound. Dylan’s fingers flew across the
strings of his Strat, every note raw, fiery, like he was pouring every
bottled-up feeling into the air. Across from him, Jaxon smirked, his Les
Paul snarling with sharp riffs that hit like daggers.


It wasn’t just a duel anymore—it was a war.

And Amelia was caught in the crossfire.


She sat between them, guitar untouched, her gaze darting back and forth.
Dylan’s style was honest, emotional—like every chord confessed something
he’d never dared to say out loud. Jaxon’s was bold, showy, dripping with
confidence, every solo demanding attention.


When Dylan ended on a soaring bend, the room broke into applause. Even
Jaxon looked impressed, though he masked it with a smirk.


“Not bad,” Jaxon said, slinging his guitar low. “Guess you’ve been
practicing instead of hiding.”


Dylan opened his mouth to retort, but Amelia spoke first.

“That was… amazing. Both of you.”


Her voice was soft but steady, and Dylan swore his heart stopped when she
looked at him like that. But then she turned to Jaxon, and her smile didn’t
fade.


“You’ve both got something special.”


Something twisted in Dylan’s chest. He wanted to believe Amelia saw him,
really saw him—but Jaxon was leaning closer, confidence radiating like heat.


“Special, huh?” Jaxon grinned at her. “Maybe you should help me write the
opener. I could use someone who actually keeps up.”


Amelia laughed nervously, tucking a strand of hair behind her ear. “Maybe…
but Dylan’s riff had something. It felt real.”


For the first time, Dylan locked eyes with Jaxon. The rivalry wasn’t just
about music anymore—it was about her.


As the meeting ended, Amelia lingered, packing her guitar slowly. Dylan
walked past, debating whether to say something—anything—when Jaxon stepped
in first.


“Hey,” Jaxon said smoothly, his voice low. “We should hang out. Just us.
Work on some songs.”


Amelia hesitated. Her eyes flicked to Dylan, then back to Jaxon.


And Dylan felt it—the start of something messy, tangled, and inescapable.


A triangle.

Music, rivalry, and a crush that could break Guitar Club apart.
Part 3

Weeks passed, and the rivalry between Dylan and Jaxon became the heartbeat
of Guitar Club.

Every rehearsal turned into a silent competition: who could hold Amelia’s
attention the longest, who could make her laugh, who could impress her with
a clever chord or lyric.


But Dylan wasn’t like Jaxon. He didn’t flirt loudly or showboat. He just…
played. Quietly, honestly, letting his guitar say what words couldn’t.


And Amelia noticed.


Sometimes, when the club broke into smaller groups, Dylan would catch her
eyes lingering on him as he bent over his guitar, coaxing melodies out of
thin air. Sometimes she’d ask, “Can you play that again?”—her voice soft,
like the song was just for her.


Jaxon noticed too. His confidence grew sharper, more possessive. He started
cornering Amelia with promises of the spotlight, telling her they could
“own the showcase” if she stuck with him.


But when Amelia sang over Dylan’s riffs during practice one day—her voice
blending perfectly with the aching chords—something shifted. Everyone in
the room went silent, goosebumps prickling their skin. The music wasn’t
flashy. It wasn’t loud. It was just… true.


And Amelia smiled at Dylan like she finally understood.

------------------------------

The night of the spring showcase, the auditorium buzzed with anticipation.
Jaxon opened strong, his performance dazzling, loud, unforgettable. The
crowd cheered, but Dylan stayed calm.


When it was his turn, he walked onstage with Amelia by his side. No big
intro. No showboating. Just two guitars and a microphone.


He started with the riff—the one he’d played the very first day of club.
This time, Amelia’s voice joined, weaving through his chords like it had
always belonged there. The melody grew, blossomed into something neither of
them could’ve made alone.


By the time the last note faded, the auditorium was silent… then erupted in
applause.


But Dylan didn’t hear the crowd.

He only heard Amelia whisper as they left the stage, her hand brushing his:


“I knew you had this in you.”


His chest tightened—not a victory over Jaxon, not even the applause. Just
her, standing there, choosing to sing with him.


It wasn’t a confession, not yet. But in the slow burn of glances, shared
songs, and unspoken words, Dylan felt it—the start of something real.


And for now, that was enough.
